skill.name: running-tests
skill.description: Runs project tests, reports failures, analyzes likely causes, and reruns verification after fixes.
skill.summary: Detects and runs project test commands, then summarizes failures and verification results.
skill.author: 1gy

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby or shell backtick execution are false positives because SKILL.md lines 16-21 contain Markdown command examples, not executable code. The weak cryptographic algorithm finding at SKILL.md line 3 is also a false positive because it is YAML description metadata. The skill still carries medium risk because it instructs agents to run project-defined test commands through Bash and may fix files or create commits.

medium_findings.title: Project Test Commands Execute Repository Code
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run detected commands such as npm test, cargo test, make test, pytest, and go test. This is expected for a testing skill, but these commands can execute arbitrary project-defined code in untrusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly listed and the skill tells the agent to run the detected command. The behavior is legitimate for the skill purpose, but the repository controls what many test commands execute.

medium_findings.title: Automated Fix and Commit Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to fix implementation or test bugs and report additional commits. This can modify repository files and commit changes, so users should review changes before accepting them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly mention fixes and commits. The exact editing path depends on the host agent, so this is a workflow risk rather than direct malicious code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Command Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at SKILL.md lines 16-21. These lines are a Markdown table of test command examples, not executable script content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside a Markdown table and use inline code formatting. There is no script file, interpolation, or runtime execution syntax present in the skill file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at SKILL.md line 3. The line is the YAML description field and contains no cryptographic function, hash algorithm, or encryption instruction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line is metadata markup only. No evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic usage was found in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash-Based Test Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is allowed to use Bash and instructs the agent to run detected test commands. This enables normal verification workflows but can run untrusted repository scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Bash is explicitly allowed and test command execution is the core workflow. The risk comes from project-controlled scripts, not from hidden malicious code in the skill.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when test commands differ across projects and failure reports are incomplete. This skill detects common test commands, runs verification, and summarizes failures with next actions.
actual_capabilities: Checks CLAUDE.md for a project-specific test command.
Detects common test commands from package.json, Cargo.toml, justfile, Makefile, pyproject.toml, and go.mod.
Asks the user for a test command when detection fails.
Runs the detected test command and reports pass or fail status.
Reports failed test names and error messages when available.
Analyzes failures, applies fixes when appropriate, and reruns tests.
limitations: It depends on local project files to infer the correct command.
It can run project-defined scripts, so untrusted repositories need review first.
It does not replace full CI coverage or environment setup.
It may need user input for uncommon build systems.

use_cases.title: Verify a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Run the expected test command, inspect failures, and summarize whether the change is ready.

use_cases.title: Triage Local Test Failures
use_cases.description: Capture failed test names and error messages, then identify whether the cause is code, tests, or environment.

use_cases.title: Standardize Repository Checks
use_cases.description: Use common project files to pick a test command when a repository lacks clear instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Run the Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner verification for an existing project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the project tests and tell me whether they pass. If you need a command, ask me first.

prompt_templates.title: Find the Test Command
prompt_templates.scenario: A repository has no obvious test instructions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Detect the right test command for this project, explain how you found it, then run it.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Failures
prompt_templates.scenario: A test run has failed and needs triage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the tests, list failed test names, summarize the errors, and identify the most likely cause.

prompt_templates.title: Fix and Reverify
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the tests, fix implementation or test issues when appropriate, rerun verification, and report any commits made.

output_examples.input: Run the tests for this Node project.
output_examples.output: Detected package.json and used npm test. The run failed in two login tests. The failures point to changed validation behavior.

output_examples.input: Check whether my Go changes are safe.
output_examples.output: Detected go.mod and ran the Go test suite. All tests passed, and no follow-up fixes were needed.

output_examples.input: The tests fail after my refactor. Help me triage.
output_examples.output: The failure is an implementation bug, not an environment issue. The affected test expects the old response shape.

best_practices: Review test scripts before running this skill in an untrusted repository.
Keep the project-specific test command documented in CLAUDE.md when possible.
Inspect file changes and commits before merging any automated fix.
anti_patterns: Do not run unknown test scripts from untrusted code without review.
Do not treat a local passing test run as a full CI replacement.
Do not commit generated fixes without reviewing the diff.

faq.question: How does the skill choose a test command?
faq.answer: It first checks CLAUDE.md, then looks for common project files such as package.json, Cargo.toml, Makefile, pyproject.toml, and go.mod.

faq.question: What happens when no command is detected?
faq.answer: The skill asks the user which test command to run.

faq.question: Can it fix failing tests?
faq.answer: Yes, it can analyze failures and apply fixes when the cause is an implementation or test issue.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Test commands can execute repository scripts, so review them before running this skill on unknown code.

faq.question: Does it support CI systems?
faq.answer: It supports local verification patterns that often match CI checks, but it does not configure CI pipelines.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
